summary:
The Second Temple of Jerusalem (516 BC – 70 AD) was the Jewish holy temple rebuilt after the Babylonian exile on the site of Solomon's First Temple, serving as the religious center of Judaism for nearly six centuries — a period that saw the crystallization of Jewish monotheism, the writing of most of the Hebrew Bible, and the emergence of both Christianity and Rabbinic Judaism from its shadow.

The Second Temple was initially a modest structure built by returnees from Babylonian exile under Persian sponsorship. [[event:1154|Herod the Great]] massively renovated and expanded it between 20 BC and 64 AD, creating one of the ancient world's most magnificent religious complexes — described by contemporaries as one of the greatest buildings of the age, with walls of white stone visible from great distances. The Temple precincts served not only as a place of sacrifice and prayer but as a focal point of Jewish communal life, pilgrimage destination, and treasury. Its destruction by the Roman general Titus in 70 AD during the suppression of the First Jewish Revolt was catastrophic for Judaism, making animal sacrifice impossible and forcing the religion to transform around synagogue prayer and scriptural study — the foundation of Rabbinic Judaism that has continued to the present.
